data.title: Top consumer discretionary gainers & losers amid tariff turbulence: Caesars Entertainment gains, CarMax bottoms
data.description:
The S&P 500 Consumer Discretionary sector (NYSEARCA:XLY) experienced a dramatic surge of over 11% this Wednesday, its best day since 2020, triggered by President Trump's decision to temporarily pause new tariffs for non-retaliatory nations.

For the week, S&P 500 Consumer Discretionary sector (NYSEARCA:XLY) has seen a rise of 5.03%, slightly lower than the S&P 500's (SP500) 5.95% rise. However, the sector's year-to-date performance is considerably weaker, down 15.44%, which is notably below the benchmark S&P 500's (SP500) decline of 8.81%.

Let’s take a look at this week’s gainers and losers in consumer discretionary as tariff repercussions continue.

Top gainers 

  • Caesars Entertainment (NASDAQ:CZR) +9.28% 
  • MGM Resorts International (NYSE:MGM) +9.27%
  • Ross Stores (NASDAQ:ROST) +8.59% after Wells Fargo upgraded the stock rating from Equal Weight to Overweight and raised the price target from $140 to $150, considering market down cycles' effects on retail.
  • Amazon.com (NASDAQ:AMZN) +8.11% after it announced that its advanced AI model, Nova Sonic, capable of understanding nuanced aspects of conversations. Meanwhile, Chinese sellers on Amazon are considering price hikes or market exit due to significant US tariffs. CEO Andy Jassy reiterated Amazon's customer-centric focus and highlighted AI investments in his shareholder letter. Furthermore, in response to tariffs, China-based Anker has already increased prices on many of its Amazon-listed products.
  • Royal Caribbean Cruises (NYSE:RCL) +7.91%

Top losers:

  • CarMax (NYSE:KMX) -9.72% after RBC Capital Markets, Needham, and Baird analysts all lowered their price targets for CarMax stock following the company's recent quarterly earnings that missed expectations. RBC Capital Markets reduced its target to $80 (from $103), Needham to $92 (from $101), and Baird to $90 (from $95). Despite these lowered targets, all three firms maintained a positive rating on the stock (Outperform or Buy).
  • Aptiv (NYSE:APTV) -9.58% after UBS downgraded Aptiv from Buy to Neutral over trade policy concerns, RBC lowered its price target to $60 but kept an outperform rating, as Aptiv's stock hit a 52-week low of $47.65 due to various challenges.
  • PulteGroup (NYSE:PHM) -6.63% after Barclays reduced its price target for PHM to $117 from $125, while maintaining an Equal Weight rating on the stock. The analyst noted that the company's initial margin outlook for 2025 carries downside risk, which could further pressure future earnings expectations.
  • D.R. Horton (NYSE:DHI) -6.33% after Barclays reduced the price target on D.R. Horton (NYSE:DHI) to $120 from $145 and maintains an Equal Weight rating ahead of Q1 earnings for homebuilding.
  • Lennar Corporation (NYSE:LEN) -5.21% after Wells Fargo cut Lennar's price target from $120 to $110, updating its builder web scrape with early April 2025 data on 4,000 units in 8 Sunbelt markets, while maintaining an Equal Weight rating.

Furthermore, for investors desiring to track the performance of the consumer discretionary sector, ETFs represent a viable alternative. 

Notable Consumer Discretionary ETFs: (NYSEARCA:XLY), (NYSEARCA:VCR), (NYSEARCA:FXD), (NYSEARCA:FDIS), (NYSEARCA:RSPD), and (NYSEARCA:RXI).

More on related stocks:

  • Ross Stores gains after Wells Fargo calls it a defensive standout amid crumbling economic data
  • Chinese electronics firm Anker raises prices on Amazon in response to tariffs: report
  • Amazon sellers will pass tariff costs on to customers: CEO
  • Chinese sellers on Amazon mull price hikes or US exit as Trump slaps 125% tariff
  • Lennar declares $0.50 dividend

data.title: Asia’s growth risks are skewed to downside: Morgan Stanley economist
data.description:
Chetan Ahya, chief Asia economist at Morgan Stanley, described the past week of volatile global markets as “the most stressful” of his career, citing escalating trade tensions and deepening uncertainty around U.S. tariffs as major risks to global and regional growth.

In an April 12 note to clients, Ahya said that while the U.S. has temporarily paused tariffs on countries excluding China, the persistent lack of clarity is already weighing on corporate confidence and could result in a sharp pullback in capital spending and trade.

“The lesson from the 2018–19 tariff experience is that slowing capex and trade are key drags on growth,” he warned.

U.S.-China tensions  

Meaningful improvement in the outlook hinges on reducing that uncertainty — potentially through deals that lower tariffs, he said. However, he also sees little near-term prospect for a U.S.-China agreement.

“We do not see an obvious way to start negotiations,” he noted, adding that both countries would likely pursue a comprehensive and time-consuming deal, complicated by strategic competition in technology, food and energy.

Other Asian economies may fare better. Japan, Korea and India are likely to offer increased purchases of U.S. goods and defense spending, which could help them secure deals and reduce bilateral trade gaps. In contrast, Vietnam may face greater difficulty given its large surplus with the U.S.

Ahya also cautioned that the temporary tariff pause could lapse before some countries secure agreements. Freight bookings between China and the U.S. have already fallen by two-thirds, signaling near-term trade disruptions.

“With uncertainty likely to persist, corporate confidence could continue to ebb, leading to softer capex and slower growth across Asia,” Ahya said. “While policy easing may follow, it likely won’t be enough to counter the drag.”

data.title: Market fatigue, fragility persist in U.S. stocks despite weekly gain: Goldman Sachs
data.description:
Despite a chaotic trading environment, U.S. equities managed to notch strong gains last week, with the S&P 500 (SP500) climbing 5.7%, according to Goldman Sachs Prime Brokerage. However, market participants remained wary, with thin sentiment support from the 90-day tariff pause and continued fragility in U.S. Treasury markets exposing deeper investor unease.

“It didn’t feel like it, but U.S. stocks finished up on the week,” Goldman analysts said in an April 11 report. They described an environment where traders are fatigued by volatility and increasingly complex crosscurrents. While themes tied to artificial intelligence, megacap tech, and defense led gains, China ADRs, oil and renewables lagged behind.

Hedge funds cover shorts, reenter tech

Goldman’s Prime data showed the largest net buying of U.S. equities since November, driven by a wave of short covering in macro products and renewed long buying in single stocks. Hedge funds reversed roughly one-third of the previous week’s macro selling and returned to sectors such as technology, media and cyclicals. Defensive sectors such as healthcare and staples were among the most sold.

Net leverage among U.S. fundamental long/short funds climbed 3.2 percentage points to 45.2%, still hovering near five-year lows. “Managers rotated into tech and cyclicals and out of defensives,” the note said.

Massive short covering on Wednesday

Last Wednesday’s rally triggered a notable shift in flows. Long-only investors and hedge funds were net buyers by $10 billion and $4.5 billion, respectively, as asset managers chased tech and AI stocks previously sold off.

“The notional U.S. short covering on Wednesday was the largest since August 2015 and the fourth-largest in the past 15 years,” Goldman noted.

Dividend futures signal growth concerns

In the futures market, dividend expectations collapsed. On Monday, dividend futures for December 2027 (ASDZ27) dropped 13% from the prior month, now reflecting zero to negative dividend growth through at least 2032. The move exceeded the SVB-driven drawdown of 2023, though it remains well short of the Covid-19 selloff in early 2020.

Dividend futures are exchange-traded derivative contracts that give investors a way to take positions on future dividend payments. The futures can be on a single company, a group of companies or on an equity index.

Derivatives, ETF flows reflect cautious optimism

Despite prevailing bearishness, Goldman’s derivatives desk sees opportunity in owning short-dated upside calls — particularly in consumer stocks with China exposure — in the event of a market squeeze or a breakthrough in U.S.-China relations.

ETF activity also underscored the market’s defensive posture. Investors favored broad market ETFs like (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:SPLG) and (NASDAQ:QQQ) while unwinding positions in sector-specific funds, including semiconductors (NASDAQ:SOXX), financials (NYSEARCA:XLF) (NYSEARCA:KBE) and energy (NYSEARCA:XLE) (NYSEARCA:XOP).

Sector breakdown: Financials, healthcare in focus

Financials kicked off earnings season with reports from JPMorgan (JPM), Morgan Stanley (MS), Wells Fargo (WFC) and others. While results landed largely in line with expectations, Goldman noted “amplified anxiety” in pre-market trade and persistent concerns ahead of regional bank earnings.

In healthcare, policy uncertainty dominated. Pharma names faced tariff-related headwinds following comments from President Trump, while managed care stocks rallied on stronger-than-expected Medicare Advantage reimbursement rates. Biotech and R&D-heavy sectors continued to face pressure, exacerbated by turmoil at the Food and Drug Administration and the Department of Health and Human Services.

Valuation and sentiment outlook

Cyclicals versus defensives — excluding commodities — have fallen 22% from year-to-date highs. Goldman said further downside is possible but sees 14% upside potential if tariffs prove less disruptive and growth sentiment turns optimistic.

Goldman’s view suggests that while equities closed the week with impressive gains, investor caution remains entrenched, positioning is still historically low and any shift in policy or macro tone could spark sharp moves in either direction.

data.title: Industrial Weekly Movers: Huntington Ingalls takes lead, Stanley Black & Decker lags
data.description:
The Wall Street continued to respond to President Donald Trump’s tariff announcements, ending the volatile week on a positive note. Earlier in the week, markets cheered after Trump announced a 90-day pause on his planned reciprocal tariffs for more than 75 countries. However, tensions with China were high where the U.S. remained locked in a tit-for-tat trade war.

The industrial sector’s movement this week was also dominated by executive orders signed by Trump mainly targeting the shipbuilding and defense industries. The sector, depicted by the Industrial Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLI) rose 5.20% in the past week, compared to the broader market (S&P500) gain of ~4%.

Weekly top 5 gainers

  • Huntington Ingalls Industries (NYSE:HII) was the biggest gainer this week with shares +16.65% after Trump signed an executive order to revive America’s shipbuilding industry and countering China’s maritime dominance.
  • Boeing (NYSE:BA) ended the week +14.83% after March jet deliveries came in at 41, compared to 29 last year. Q1 deliveries stood at 130 jets, up from 83 in Q1 2024. However, the company was temporarily pressured after China’s Juneyao Airlines delayed the delivery of a 787-9 Dreamliner aircraft, valued at ~$120M, because of Trump's tariffs on China.
  • Axon Enterprise (NASDAQ:AXON) was +14.25% in the week ended April 11.
  • United Airlines (NASDAQ:UAL) ended in the green this week with its shares +13.75%.
  • Eaton (NYSE:ETN) closed +12.58% this week.

Weekly top 5 losers

  • Stanley Black & Decker (NYSE:SWK) saw its shares drown, closing -6.15% this week.
  • Builders FirstSource (NYSE:BLDR) ended the week -2.47%.
  • Masco (NYSE:MAS) ended the week -1.56%.
  • Generac Holdings (NYSE:GNRC) was -1.44%. Earlier in the week, the company introduced a lineup of five new generators for the North American market.
  • J.B. Hunt Transport Services (NASDAQ:JBHT) ended in the red, with its shares -1.39%.

ETFs to tab the industrial sector include: (XLI), (NYSEARCA:VIS), (NYSEARCA:XAR), (NYSEARCA:EXI), (NASDAQ:PSCI), (NASDAQ:PRN), (NYSEARCA:FIDU).

data.title: Commerce Secretary Lutnick calls tariff exemptions for electronic products temporary
data.description:
Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick said on Sunday the administration's decision to exclude smartphones, computers and other popular electronic items from reciprocal tariffs was just a temporary reprieve. 

The items will be subject to "semiconductor tariffs," expected to be implemented in "a month or two," Lutnick said in an interview with ABC. "All those products are going to come under semiconductors, and they're going to have a special focus type of tariff to make sure that those products get reshored." 

President Donald Trump's administration on Friday exempted a host of consumer electronic devices from reciprocal tariffs that include 125% tariffs on Chinese imports and 10% baseline tariffs on other countries.

The exclusions are seen as a major win for American tech players like Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL), Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) and Dell (NYSE:DELL).

"He's saying they're exempt from the reciprocal tariffs, but they're included in the semiconductor tariffs, which are coming in probably a month or two. So these are coming soon," Lutnick added. 

"The mass confusion created by this constant news flow out of the White House is dizzying for the industry and investors and creating massive uncertainty and chaos for companies trying to plan their supply chain, inventory, and demand," said Wedbush analyst Dan Ives. 

Trump on Saturday said he will provide an update on the administration's proposed semiconductor tariffs on Monday. ""I'll give you that answer on Monday," Trump told reporters aboard Air Force One." 

In February, Trump suggested tariffs in the "in the neighborhood of 25%" on autos and similar duties on semiconductors and pharmaceutical imports.

"We are in a much better spot than Friday and last week heading into this Sunday night....but still there is mass uncertainty, chaos, and confusion about the next steps ahead with all focus on China tariff negotiations being front and center," said Ives.

More on Apple, Analog Devices, etc.

data.title: Apple's iPhone production in India rises 60%: report
data.description:
Apple’s (NASDAQ:AAPL) iPhone production in India rose 60% in the 12 months through March to $22B, Bloomberg reported late Saturday, citing people familiar with the matter. 

According to the report, the company now produces 20% of its iPhones in the country, the people said. 

Bloomberg said the dollar amount is the iPhone’s factory gate value, and not the retail price. 

The report said most of the iPhones assembly in India are produced by Foxconn Technology (OTCPK:FXCOF) at its factory in the country’s south. Tata Group is also a major supplier, the report added. 

The people reportedly said Apple has increased shipments of the devices from India after US President Donald Trump in February announced his plans for “reciprocal” tariffs. The company’s Indian production and exports spiked throughout the fiscal year through month, Bloomberg reported.  

The Trump administration late Friday exempted smartphones, computers and other electronics, mostly imported from China.

data.title: U.S. HVAC shipments show mixed trends in February, water heaters decline
data.description:
U.S. shipments of residential and commercial water heaters saw notable year-over-year declines in February 2025, while gas furnace shipments surged, according to data released by the Air-Conditioning, Heating and Refrigeration Institute.

Water heater shipments decline

Residential gas storage water heater shipments dropped 5.3% to 376,403 units, down from 397,529 units in February 2024. Shipments of residential electric storage models also declined, falling 6.4% to 425,332 units compared with 454,577 units a year earlier.

 

 

In the commercial segment, gas storage water heater shipments decreased 5.7% to 7,660 units from 8,120 the previous year. Commercial electric storage models fell 3.9%, totaling 13,409 units versus 13,950 in February 2024.

Furnace shipments surge

In contrast, shipments of warm air furnaces posted strong gains. Gas furnace shipments jumped 20.7% year-over-year to 259,405 units, up from 214,961. Oil furnace shipments also rose significantly, increasing 20.4% to 2,931 units from 2,434 the previous year.

Cooling equipment shows mixed performance

Total U.S. shipments of central air conditioners and air-source heat pumps declined 3.2% to 636,302 units in February 2025, down from 657,509 units a year earlier.

Air conditioner shipments were the main contributor to the decline, falling 8.1% to 342,542 units. However, air-source heat pump shipments grew 3.2% to 293,760 units, up from 284,612 in February 2024.

 

 

The latest figures reflect a complex demand landscape influenced by shifting weather patterns, economic factors and evolving energy efficiency preferences across residential and commercial markets.

data.title: Around 10% of world is using OpenAI's systems: Sam Altman
data.description:
OpenAI chief executive Sam Altman said on Friday the generative AI startup had reached about 800 million users. 

"Something like 10% of the world uses our systems, now a lot," Altman said to host Chris Anderson at the TED 2025 event, referring to broadly hundreds of millions of users. 

Anderson revealed that Altman had said the startup's users were growing rapidly, doubling in a "just a few weeks." 

The Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT)-backed startup's growth has received a boost from viral features, such as the recently launched ability to create images and videos in various styles, including the style of legendary Japanese animation studio, Studio Ghibli. 

On March 31, Altman said in a post on X that the company had hit one million users in five days, as users flocked to ChatGPT to try the viral Ghibli photo trend. He added that OpenAI had added a million users in one hour. 

When asked about if the company had considered compensating artists for creating works in their style, Altman said there could possibly be prompts that could trigger payments for specific artists. 

"I think it would be cool to figure out a new model where if you say 'I want to do it in the name of this artist' and they opt in, there’s a revenue model there," said Altman. 

Altman added the company had guardrails in place to prevent the AI model from generating images in the styles of specific artists or creators. 

He also spoke about the company's work on AI agents, models that can operate autonomously on behalf of users. 

OpenAI is now the world's most valuable startup, overtaking TikTok-owner ByteDance (BDNCE) with a valuation of $300 billion. Its valuation has nearly doubled since October 2024.

data.title: Notable healthcare headlines of the week: Norvatis, Novo Nordisk, Eli Lilly in focus
data.description:
Wall Street on Friday overcame a volatile morning trading session to push higher, ending a wild week on a positive note. Tariffs dominated headlines, with the latest being that the Trump administration had made "very good progress" on trade negotiations. 

The S&P 500 Health Care Index Sector (NYSEARCA:XLV) was marginally up (+0.31%) during the week.

The top S&P 500 healthcare gainers and losers for the last week are as follows:

Top Gainers:

  • Humana (HUM) +15.99%
  • UnitedHealth (UNH) +14.17%
  • DexCom (DXCM) +10.66%
  • Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) +9.51
  • CVS Health (CVS) +9.48

· Top Losers:

  • Charles River Laboratories International (CRL) -27.14%
  • Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY) -8.30%
  • Biogen (BIIB) -6.42%
  • AbbVie (ABBV) -6.37%
  • Incyte (INCY) -6.12%

Here are some of the important healthcare stories from this week:

Novartis is eyeing $23B investment in U.S.

Novartis (NVS) announced Thursday it plans to invest $23B to build and expand 10 U.S. plants to ensure that all key medicines developed by the company are manufactured domestically. The announcement comes amid President Donald Trump’s repeated threats to impose tariffs on pharmaceutical goods. The expansion is anticipated to generate over 1,000 jobs at Novartis (NVS) and roughly 4,000 additional jobs. 

J&J upgraded Pfizer, AbbVie downgraded as Goldman reviews U.S. biopharma coverage

Goldman Sachs made significant rating changes across its U.S. biopharma coverage on Tuesday, upgrading the likes of Eli Lilly (LLY) and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) while downgrading Bristol Myers (BMY), Pfizer (PEE), and AbbVie (ABBV).

Goldman Sachs analyst Asad Haider upgraded Eli Lilly (LLY) to Buy from Neutral, while decreasing its price target to $888 from $892 per share.

The brokerage firm upgraded Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) to Buy from Neutral, increasing price target to $172 from $157.

The analyst downgraded AbbVie (ABBV) to Neutral from Buy and trimmed its 12-month price target to $194 from $212 per share.

Goldman downgraded Pfizer (PEF) to Neutral from Buy and lowered its 12-month price target to $25 from $32. 

The firm also downgraded Bristol Myers (BMY) to Neutral from Buy and trimmed its 12-month price target to $55 from $67 

Novo reportedly eyeing $1.09B investment in Brazil to expand obesity drug production

Novo Nordisk (NVO) plans to invest 6.4B reais ($1.09B) in Brazil to boost the production of its highly popular diabetes/weight loss drugs Ozempic and Wegovy, as well as other injectables targeted at chronic diseases, Reuters reported Monday.

Eli Lilly to recognize $1.57B in Q1 as acquired R&D charges

Eli Lilly (LLY) announced that its financial results for Q1 2025, prepared under the U.S. generally accepted accounting principles (GAAP), will include $1.57B recognized on a pre-tax basis as acquired In-Process Research and Development (IPR&D) charges.

The acquired IPR&D charges are expected to impact the Indiana-based pharma giant’s earnings for the quarter, creating a headwind of about $1.57 per share for both GAAP and non-GAAP earnings, LLY said in a statement on Thursday.

According to Wall Street estimates, Eli Lilly (LLY) is set to report $4.70 per share of earnings on average on revenue of $12.8B for Q1 2025.

Bristol-Myers gets Pomalyst antitrust case dismissed

A federal judge in New York has dismissed a proposed class action filed by health insurer Blue Cross Blue Shield of Louisiana alleging that Bristol Myers Squibb (BMY) ran an illegal scheme to protect its monopoly over its blockbuster multiple myeloma treatment Pomalyst.

U.S. District Judge Edgardo Ramos in Manhattan dismissed the case, noting that the plaintiffs had failed to establish that the company had violated the federal Sherman antitrust law, Reuters reported, citing a 70-page court document made public on Tuesday.

Novavax in selloff after RFK Jr.’s comments on COVID vaccine

Novavax (NVAX) shares sold off on Thursday after Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. cast doubts about the efficacy of the company's COVID shot amid a delay for its U.S. approval. During an interview with Dr. Jon Lapook of CBS News, RFK Jr. attributed the delay to the vaccine’s single-antigen composition, which he said has never worked against respiratory diseases.
